headline: Video comparison complete.
dek: The two files are compared as A versus B cortical response estimates. Use the track deltas and brain view to inspect where the difference is concentrated.

a.label: A
a.name: control_cut.mp4
a.summary: Lower early attention, slower product reveal, and a steadier closing window.

scenes.title: Cold open
scenes.beat: Hands enter frame before context lands.
scenes.visual: Soft beige desk, slow camera drift

scenes.title: Proof
scenes.beat: The product appears after the setup has settled.

scenes.title: Human read
scenes.beat: A face reaction carries the middle section.

b.label: B
b.name: active_cut.mp4
b.summary: Higher early attention, earlier face cue, and a stronger closing contrast.

scenes.title: Instant hook
scenes.beat: Motion and face appear in the first second.

scenes.title: Tension
scenes.beat: The object and person alternate quickly.
scenes.visual: Jump cuts, red detail in frame

scenes.title: Payoff
scenes.beat: The strongest social cue sits before the product claim.

scenes.title: Hard close
scenes.beat: The call-to-action lands with motion still active.

moments.title: Early attention shift
moments.detail: B is higher on the attention track in the opening window.
moments.move: Treat this as a cortical contrast signal, not a quality score.

moments.title: Midpoint social shift
moments.detail: B is higher on the social track around the reaction-shot window.
moments.move: Treat this as a cortical contrast signal, not a quality score.

moments.title: Closing memory shift
moments.detail: A is higher on the memory track near the closing window.
moments.move: Treat this as a cortical contrast signal, not a quality score.

recommendations: Largest positive shift: B attention and motion in the opening windows.
Largest negative shift: A memory near the closing window.
Review the cortical surface and timing windows before selecting a version.
